risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A list was produced by the Nottingham Clinical Trials Unit to allow simple (unrestricted) randomization (Schulz & Grimes, 2002). ... If participants consented, they were randomized by the portal designers at ANU." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation process using an unrestricted randomization list generated by a clinical trials unit and implemented through the study website/portal. The wording is sufficiently specific to support a truly random sequence, so Low risk is appropriate.

_evidence.method_quote: A list was produced by the Nottingham Clinical Trials Unit to allow simple (unrestricted) randomization (Schulz & Grimes, 2002). Once potential participants had completed the screening questions, if eligible for inclusion in the trial, they were given a study ID, allocated through the website, and they were then invited to join the trial. If participants consented, they were randomized by the portal designers at ANU.
_evidence.method_summary: Simple unrestricted randomization was used, with allocation implemented through the study website/portal.
context_clues: randomized by the portal designers at ANU

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "allocated through the website... If participants consented, they were randomized by the portal designers at ANU. In this way the randomization status of participants was concealed from their employers and from the research team until the study was completed." Comment: This describes a central, website-based allocation process that prevented recruiters/employers/research team from foreseeing the next assignment, which is sufficient to judge allocation concealment as low risk.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Once potential participants had completed the screening questions, if eligible for inclusion in the trial, they were given a study ID, allocated through the website, and they were then invited to join the trial. If participants consented, they were randomized by the portal designers at ANU. In this way the randomization status of participants was concealed from their employers and from the research team until the study was completed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches, and researcher bias was avoided completely because most data for the outcome measures were collected online. However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants." Comment: Participants were explicitly told a neutral trial description and most outcome data were collected online, which makes knowledge of allocation unlikely to have influenced the main patient-reported outcomes; the only unblinded personnel were telephone interviewers for service use/costing, not the core outcomes. Overall this supports low risk for performance bias across the study outcomes.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches, and researcher bias was avoided completely because most data for the outcome measures were collected online. However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants.
_evidence.blinding_method: Participants were told they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches; most outcome data were collected online. Telephone interviewers for service use measures were not blind.
outcome_categories: sick leave / days off due to ill-health
subjective_outcomes: sick leave / days off due to ill-health

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches, and researcher bias was avoided completely because most data for the outcome measures were collected online. However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants." Comment: The outcome assessment was mixed: most key outcomes (WSAS, PHQ-9, CORE10, GAD, EQ-5D, self-assessed absence from work) were patient-reported online, so the participant is the assessor. The paper does not establish that participants were actually blinded, only that the study aimed to be double-blind and that most data were collected online. Because self-reported subjective outcomes are vulnerable to knowledge of allocation, and one outcome domain was explicitly assessed by unblinded telephone interviewers, the worst-outcome rule leads to an overall High risk judgement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches, and researcher bias was avoided completely because most data for the outcome measures were collected online. However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "There were 9305 visits to the website, 1111 completed the screening and 637 were randomized, with 359 completing 6-week online assessments and 231 participants completing 12-week online assessments." Comment: This shows substantial post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcomes, with only 36% completing 12-week assessment overall and a clear imbalance noted in the article: "more participants were lost to follow-up from the MoodGYM arm than from the control arm, although this was not significant." The paper also states that "at the end of the study when we could see that attrition was very large" and, although an ITT mixed-effects model was used, the observed loss exceeds the high-risk threshold and is differential across arms.

per_arm_flow.reasons: Table 2: 'Control (n = 319)'; Table 3 gives analysed n at each time point: WSAS baseline 319, 6 weeks 188, 12 weeks 129; PHQ-9 baseline 318, 6 weeks 176, 12 weeks 122; CORE10 baseline 318, 6 weeks 187, 12 weeks 129; GAD baseline 305, 6 weeks 181, 12 weeks 123.

Table 2: 'MoodGYM (n = 318)'; Table 3 gives analysed n at each time point: WSAS baseline 317, 6 weeks 171, 12 weeks 102; PHQ-9 baseline 311, 6 weeks 164, 12 weeks 97; CORE10 baseline 316, 6 weeks 171, 12 weeks 101; GAD baseline 303, 6 weeks 166, 12 weeks 98.

_evidence.missing_data_method: intention-to-treat; linear mixed-effect model for longitudinal data (random intercept model) using maximum likelihood; all participants with outcome data available were included; no imputation was conducted on outcome scores.
_evidence.notes: Attrition is reported for the primary outcome WSAS over 6 and 12 weeks. The article states 637 randomized, 359 completed 6-week assessments, and 231 completed 12-week assessments; the 64% total loss is based on 12-week completion. The article also says 'more participants were lost to follow-up from the MoodGYM arm than from the control arm, although this was not significant' but does not give explicit dropout reasons tied to efficacy or adverse events.
